docs.title: About me
docs.text:
I am an engineering physics student based in Vietnam. My research interest encompasses solid state physics, but mostly focuses in atomistic modelling of materials for clean energy generation and storage development.

This portfolio records my professional journey, as well as my self-exploratory projects as an enthusiast.

docs.title: Duy M. Dao
docs.text: Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam · daoduy.work@gmail.com

docs.title: Education
docs.text:
Vietnam National University - Ho Chi Minh University of Technology Ho Chi Minh city, Vietnam B.Sc in Engineering Physics 2022 - 2026

GPA: 3.4/4.0

Relevant coursework: General Physics I & II, General Chemistry, Numerical Methods, Fundamentals of Quantum Mechanics & and Solid State Physics, Fundamentals of Nuclear Physics & Applications, Engineering Optics & Applications, Physical Chemistry I & II (undertaking), Colloidal Chemistry (undertaking).

docs.title: Research Experience
docs.text:
Research Center for Applied Science, Academia Sinica Remote and on-site in Taipei, Taiwan Intern Researcher March 2026 - Present

Supervisor: Dr. Chao-Cheng Kuan

  • Utilized Density Functional Theory (DFT) to model the Ammonia Oxidation Reaction (AOR) on transition metal-doped Nickel Oxide (NiO) surfaces, with the objective of designing more efficient catalysts for direct ammonia fuel cells.

  • Analyzed the effect of dopants (V, Cr, Fe, Mn) on the electronic structure of NiO, correlating modifications in \(Ni\) \(3d–O 2p\) hybridization with the energetic stabilization of the crucial \(∗NH_2\) reaction intermediate.

  • Calculated and compared the free energy barriers for the potential-determining dehydrogenation step (\(∗NH_3 \rightarrow *NH_2\)) to screen and identify dopant candidates that accelerate the overall AOR kinetics.

Institute of Ion-beam Physics and Material Research, Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf Dresden, Germany Intern Researcher June 2025 - September 2025

Supervisor: Dr. Mahdi Ghorbani-Asl

  • Studied, conducted research, attended lectures and lab tours with a working time of 39 hours/week.

  • Developed theory framework for interface formation and stress relaxation mechanism of 2D lateral hetero-structures.

  • Pioneered in utilizing Molecular Dynamics to investigate interface energetics of 2D lateral hetero-structure.

HCMUT Laboratory of Computational Physics, Vietnam National University - Ho Chi Minh University of Technology Ho Chi Minh city, Vietnam Student Researcher December 2023 - Present

Supervisor: Dr. Tran Thi Thu Hanh

  • Conducted first principle study on hydrogenation of doped 2D Silicon Carbide for energy storage application.

  • Studied and conducted literature reviews on 2D materials for energy storage and conversion applications.

  • Gained hands-on experience using Molecular Dynamics (LAMMPS) and Density Functional Theory (SIESTA) softwares.

docs.title: Miscellaneous and frequently asked questions (FAQs)
docs.text: This page is dedicated to new undergrads, especially Vietnamese, who aspire to pursue a research career.

docs.title: Research Experience for Undergraduates (REU)
docs.text: I can't stress enough the importance of REU if you want to pursue a research career. In fact, in my experience, REU and recommendation from your principal investigators (PIs) and academic supervisors outweigh your GPA (a competittive GPA still helps even if it's not tantamount). Here are some programs that I have applied. They are all fully-funded and have computational physics/chemistry positions. You can read the information in detail directly at their website. Here, I will just provide the insights to apply for those programs if there are any.

docs.title: Germany
docs.text:
Germany has two prominent research institutes with summer program for international undergraduate students: Helmholtz Zentrum and Max Planck Institute.

  • Helmholtz Zentrum Dresden Rossendorf (HZDR) and Berlin (HZB) Summer Program: Relevancy is key. Supervisors usually choose candidates whose research interest and experience align with them instead of looking at your GPA. My advice is to look through your prefered department, scan through the PIs list, and read their latest researches thoroughly to tailor your profile accordingly. This can actually apply to everywhere and anything.

  • Max Planck Institute: If you don't have a 3.6/4.0, you needn't bother applying for this. If you do, you still need prior experience in your home university's lab to compete. In short, absolute academic excellence is mandatory.

docs.title: Finland
docs.text: • Aalto Science Institute (AScI) International Summer Research Programme: It's best that you display proficient english during interview since Finnish employers, in general, prefer to work with people whom they can communicate easily. It's very competitive - over 7000 applicants in 2025 and 5000 in 2026. This is the one program that I have to stress how important language proficiency is.

docs.title: Taiwan
docs.text:
• Taiwan International Internship Program (TIIP): Those are essentially pre-doctoral level program. Extensive prior experience is a must.

  • International Internship Piloting Program (IIPP)

  • Taiwan Education Exchance Program (TEEP)

docs.title: Japan
docs.text:
• University of Tokyo Summer Internship Program (UTSIP): A strong GPA is a strong starting point since PIs' requirements on experience for interns are generally low. Regardless, a solid background on the research makes things easier.

  • Univeristy of Tokyo Research Internship Program (UTRIP): Quite literallly the Asian's counterpart of Max Planck institute.

docs.title: Graduate Programs and Scholarships
docs.text: Here, I only introduce programs for computational physics/chemistry and material science. Again, You can read the information in detail directly at their website. I also want to stress that global ranking doesn't mean everything: It comprises of many metrics, some of which are inconsequential to the actual learning experience and outcomes. The important things are: Stipends, Coursework, and your PIs. All of the programs below are either fully-funded or have external grants that you can apply.

docs.title: European Union - Erasmus_Mundus Joint Master Degree (EMJMD)
docs.text:
You will study in different schools belonged to a consortium in each semester. The stipend is really good of 1400 euros. Since you have to compete globally, the difficulty need not be discussed.

  • EMJMD in Theoretical Chemistry and Computational Modeling (TCCM): This one required extensive coursework including physical chemistry, quantum mechanics, thermodynamics, statistical mechanics, ... along with background on computational chemistry methods and theories such as Density Functional Theory (DFT), Molecular Dynamics (MD), ... The payoff is that the consortium comprises of prestigious schools in the field and a dream-like cirriculum for theorist.

  • Erasmus-Mundus Master in Nanoscience and Nanotechnology (EMM-NANO): It has a modeling track based in Uiversity of Barcelona. A strong GPA (preferably above 3.2/4.0) with some REUs are sufficient.

docs.title: China - China Scholarship Council (CSC)
docs.text: • Tsinghua University - International Master Program of Material Science and Engineering (IMP-MSE): A decent GPA and a working knowledge on any fields regarding material science are sufficient. They have a big council for interview: The questions are heavily oriented toward AI integration into material science and authenticating your research experience.

docs.title: France
docs.text: • Paris Sciences et Lettres (PSL) - PhD Track in Chemistry: Their cirriculum are heavily oriented toward computation and modeling despite a very general name of "Chemistry". An excellent GPA (atleast 3.6/4.0) and extensive coursework in chemistry and physics is a mch. Otherwise, you need not bother applying.

docs.title: Taiwan
docs.text: • Academia Sinica - Taiwan Internationa Graduate Program: It requires 3 recommnedations. One of them has to be that of your academic supervisor. Participating in TIIP is an advantage since you can get quality recommendations from within the institute.

docs.title: Germany - German Academic Exchange Service (DAAD)
docs.text:
The thing about German graduate program is that: If you come from Vietnam, China, and India, you have to authenticate your diplomas (both bachelor and highschool) through German Embassy to get a certificate, called APS. The authenication process takes very long. There are two cycles per year. You have to submit your documents 3 months prior to the interviews, which take place in May and November, and wait another 1 month to get your certificate. The tricky part is that admission cycles usually end before May and November, so you have to prepare this in advance, preferrably 6 months early.

  • Georg-August-Universität Göttingen - Master in Chemsitry : Its ranking seems normal, but it was quite literally the birth place of quantum mechanics, or modern physics in general: It's where Bohr, Heisenberg, and Oppenheimer worked. I don't think I need to elaborate further.

  • Friedrich-Schiller-Universität Jena - Master in Chemistry

docs.title: Hungary - Stipendium Hungaricum
docs.text:
Hungary is such an underdog - it has a long-standing history of physics and science in general, affordable life, and top-tier institutes. Edward Teller, Leo Szilard, Rudorf Ortvay, John von Neumann, ... are all Hungarian. This scholarship covers almost everything: accomodation, insurance, along with some stipend.

  • Eötvös Loránd University (ELTE) - MSc in Materials Science: ELTE is arguably the top science university in Hungary and has a powerhouse in theoretical and computational chemistry. Its Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry is one of the most prominent groups in Europe. They are particularly famous for their work in high-accuracy quantum chemistry, development of theoretical methods, and computational spectroscopy.

  • Budapest University of Technology and Economics (BME) - MSc in Materials Engineering

  • University of Debrecen - MSc in Materials Science

docs.title: VISA application for REUs
docs.text:
General tips

  • Please keep any notarized translation of your documents! You may need it for future application. They are unexpectedly expensive.

  • Have a plus-1 of every document that you print - An original one, a copied one, and a back-up one.

docs.title: Germany
docs.text: For Vietnamese students, following the instruction from the embassy is sufficient. However, you have to start early since it can take up to 1 month to obtain your visa. One important thing is that you have to translate your highschool diploma if it's required, and get it notarized.

docs.title: Taiwan
docs.text:
Of course, It requires your notarized translation of your available diploma, which is highschool one if you are still an undergrad. This time, you need to get that notarized diploma authenticated by the Department of External Affair (6 Alexander de Rhodes street, District 1).

Moreover, although you will be funded, they still requires a proof of sufficient fund, which comprises of your sponsor's proof of saving account along with any documents that prove your relation with them (usually your birth certificate if your sponsor is your parent).

docs.title: First principle study of doped \(NiO_2\) to enhance amonia reduction reaction (On-going)
docs.text:
I'm working on this project alongside with my PI in Academia Sinica for my internship. On-site participation is from July to September, but we have been working on this in advance to ensure maximum output.

Ammonia oxidation reaction (AOR) is a key anodic process for direct ammonia fuel cells (DAFCs) and for converting ammonia into benign nitrogen products while generating electricity. As ammonia offers high hydrogen density and easier storage than \(H_2\), improving AOR efficiency is crucial for establishing a sustainable hydrogen–ammonia energy cycle. Building on A Density Functional Theory Investigation of Ammonia Oxidation Pathways on Nickel Oxide, which identified the first dehydrogenation step (\(*NH_3\) - \(*NH_2\), \(\Delta G = 1.25\,eV\)) as the universal potential-determining step on \(NiO(100)\), we aim to lower this barrier by transtion metal dopants. Dopants possessing multiple oxidation states, such as \(V\), \(Cr\), \(Fe\), or \(Mn\), can tune the \(Ni\) \(3d–O\, 2p\) hybridization, enhance charge delocalization, and stabilize the \(*NH_2\) intermediate, thereby accelerating the proton–electron transfer.

docs.title: An introductory book on quantum mechanics (On-going)
docs.text:
During my undergraduate studies, I encountered numerous textbooks on quantum mechanics, yet not a single one satisfied my personal demand for both mathematical rigor and physical intuition. Most university courses rely on these standard texts, leaving students with a sense of "missing pieces" that forced me to forage through online resources.

My goal is to write a book that incorporates insights such as

  • Foundation in linear algebra: Quantum mechanics should be built upon linear algebra from the very first page. Popular introductory books, such as the one by David Griffiths, often approach the subject by solving the Schrödinger equation for the wave function immediately. This approach misses the most critical insight: the wave function is a vector, and function space is a vector space. By treating the wave function as an element of a Hilbert space, the abstract operations of quantum mechanics become geometrically intuitive. Students must be comfortable with the concepts of vectors, matrices, eigenvalues, eigenvectors, change of basis, and inner products—all of which have direct, indispensable counterparts in the quantum world.

  • Refining the Subtleties: Most introductory books gloss over the "refined details" when solving simple systems, leaving gaps in a student's conceptual framework. My approach focuses on:

    • The Origin of Uncertainty: Heisenberg’s Uncertainty Principle is often taught as a mysterious "fuzziness." In reality, it is a rigorous consequence of non-commuting operators. When two operators do not commute, an eigenstate of one must be expressed as a linear combination of the eigenstates of the other. This relationship has a direct correspondence to the Fourier transform—a beautiful mathematical symmetry that is often neglected in undergraduate curricula.

    • The Paradox of Tunneling: Why doesn't quantum tunneling violate the conservation of energy? This cannot be fully understood without introducing the concept of motion integrals and quantum uncertainty. Furthermore, many students are left wondering what the "average momentum" of a particle is while it exists inside a potential barrier. My book aims to clarify these nuances by deriving qualitative physical pictures directly from mathematical rigor.

Beyond the equations, I want to incorporate visualization tools to bridge the gap between abstract math and physical reality. By leveraging modern visualization, we can show how the rigorous properties of linear operators translate into the behavior of physical systems. The ultimate objective of this project is to create a resource where the math is not just a tool for calculation, but the very source of physical intuition.

I have also dedicated an online version in english here since the book is for Vietnamese student.
